paraphrase: Sam and Gilly are attacked by wights north of the Wall and saved by a thin, gaunt man in black clothes who rides a giant elk and commands a large group of ravens to fight off the wights.
The man never gives a name; Sam calls him Coldhands because of his cold, black hands.
Coldhands takes Sam and Gilly south to the abandoned Nightfort, where it's revealed he was sent by the three-eyed crow to fetch Bran Stark.
In A Dance with Dragons, Coldhands leads Bran, Hodor, Meera, and Jojen north to reach the three-eyed crow.
Meera grows increasingly suspicious of Coldhands during the journey because he won't explain who he is or what's going on, hides his face behind a scarf and hood, and is evidently dead himself.
Coldhands doesn't eat, breathe, or seem to sleep, and his heart doesn't beat -- functioning like a wight in that sense.
Coldhands and wights are both described as dead, pale things with cold, black hands; both are unable to cross certain magical wards; and neither seems to feel cold.
Coldhands fears fire, which is known to kill wights, paralleling that vulnerability.
Unlike wights, which have bright blue eyes, Coldhands has black eyes.
Unlike wights, which are slow and clumsy, Coldhands is agile.
Unlike wights, which don't talk, Coldhands speaks in a rattling voice with intelligence and even hints of emotion.
Unlike wights, which seem to have lost their humanity, Coldhands still seems to be a person; and while wights serve the Others, Coldhands serves the three-eyed crow.

The three-eyed crow is identified as Brynden Rivers, known as Bloodraven -- a Targaryen bastard, former Lord Commander of the Night's Watch, described as a 'Dreamer, wizard... greenseer,' about 125 years old, apparently kept alive by his connection to the weirwoods and the magic of the old gods.
verbatim: “A friend. Dreamer, wizard, call him what you will. The last greenseer.” The longhall’s wooden door banged open. Outside, the night wind howled, bleak and black. The trees were full of ravens, screaming. Coldhands did not move.

paraphrase: Bloodraven's goals are shady and unclear, but he spends his time with the children of the forest, watches the world through weirwood trees, skinchanges into ravens, and manipulates events from afar.

Some fans think Bloodraven directly skinchanges into Coldhands the way Bran controls Hodor; the video allows this might happen sometimes but argues it can't be constant, since Coldhands clearly exists and acts as his own person.
Like the greenseers among the children of the forest, Coldhands has power over animals: he commands a group of ravens to scout and fight for him and can understand their calls.
verbatim: From a nearby oak a raven quorked, and Bran heard the sound of wings as another of the big black birds flapped down to land beside it. By day only half a dozen ravens stayed with them, flitting from tree to tree or riding on the antlers of the elk. The rest of the murder flew ahead or lingered behind. But when the sun sank low they would return, descending from the sky on night-black wings until every branch of every tree was thick with them for yards around. Some would fly to the ranger and mutter at him, and it seemed to Bran that he understood their quorks and squawks. They are his eyes and ears. They scout for him, and whisper to him of dangers ahead and behind.

spoken_quote: a blessing in some strange tongue as he slit
paraphrase: Coldhands rides a giant elk, and when it dies he performs a small ritual over it, murmuring a blessing in a strange tongue as he cuts its throat.
verbatim: “Jojen just needs to eat,” Bran said, miserably. It had been twelve days since the elk had collapsed for the third and final time, since Coldhands had knelt beside it in the snowbank and murmured a blessing in some strange tongue as he slit its throat. Bran wept like a little girl when the bright blood came rushing out. He had never felt more like a cripple than he did then, watching helplessly as Meera Reed and Coldhands butchered the brave beast who had carried them so far. He told himself he would not eat, that it was better to go hungry than to feast upon a friend, but in the end he’d eaten twice, once in his own skin and once in Summer’s. As gaunt and starved as the elk had been, the steaks the ranger carved from him had sustained them for seven days, until they finished the last of them huddled over a fire in the ruins of an old hillfort.

paraphrase: The video's core theory for what animates Coldhands: just as the Others resurrect wights with ice magic and Thoros resurrects Beric Dondarrion with fire magic, Bloodraven may resurrect Coldhands with nature magic, making him just another kind of zombie.
The identity question matters because Meera keeps pressing Coldhands about who he is, and he refuses to show his face or give his real name.
Coldhands calls Sam 'brother,' suggesting he was once a member of the Night's Watch.

spoken_quote: Anyone can put on a black cloak
paraphrase: Coldhands wears black like a Night's Watch brother, but Meera cautions that anyone can put on a black cloak, so his Watch membership isn't certain.
verbatim: “There’s been too much going around,” Meera insisted, “and too many secrets. I don’t like it. I don’t like him. And I don’t trust him. Those hands of his are bad enough. He hides his face, and will not speak a name. Who is he? What is he? Anyone can put on a black cloak. Anyone, or any thing. He does not eat, he never drinks, he does not seem to feel the cold.”

paraphrase: A popular fan theory held that Coldhands is Benjen Stark, Ned's brother who went beyond the Wall in Book 1 and never returned -- maybe killed by White Walkers and later resurrected by Bloodraven.

A Reddit user (_honeybird) reportedly found a note by George R.R. Martin in the original A Dance with Dragons manuscript explicitly stating Coldhands is not Benjen Stark, which the video treats as ruling out the Benjen theory.
Other possible identities include Will and Waymar Royce, Night's Watchmen killed in the Prologue of A Game of Thrones.

Since Waymar (and probably Will) were turned into wights by the Others, someone would have had to restore their mind after zombification for either to be Coldhands -- something never shown to happen in the books -- making this identity unlikely.
Coldhands was reportedly killed 'long ago' according to a roughly two-hundred-year-old child of the forest, suggesting he died many years before the story begins.

One theory holds Coldhands is Ser Duncan the Tall, protagonist of the Dunk and Egg novellas set nearly a century before the main series; Dunk had a relationship with Bloodraven, meeting him at the end of The Mystery Knight and escorting him to the Wall when Bloodraven joined the Night's Watch, so perhaps Dunk later joined the Watch himself, was killed beyond the Wall, and became Coldhands.

The video says there's no real evidence for the Duncan theory and several facts contradict it: Dunk is very tall while Coldhands apparently isn't, Dunk seems to have died at Summerhall rather than beyond the Wall, and if Dunk had joined the Night's Watch it would likely have been mentioned.

Another theory links Coldhands to the legendary Night's King, a Lord Commander from eight thousand years ago who reportedly married an Other or a wight; this would explain how Coldhands knows about the Black Gate, since the Night's King ruled at the Nightfort and may have used the Black Gate for secret sacrifices.

Against the Night's King theory: he was reportedly cast down by the wildlings and the Starks, while Coldhands was reportedly killed by the Others -- and it's unclear why the Others would kill someone who had been making sacrifices to them, or why the wildlings and Starks would have let him live given they supposedly obliterated his very name from memory.

Even setting that aside, the theory doesn't explain why the Night's King would survive as a sentient wight for eight thousand years, why he'd end up working for Bloodraven, or why he'd have power over animals.

Eight thousand years is argued to be too long for a body and mind to survive intact -- the books say cold preserves, but Coldhands is never described as unusually ancient-looking, and he moves, speaks, and fights too capably for an eight-thousand-year-old corpse.
verbatim: “. . . or not.” Aemon chuckled softly. “Or I am an old man, feverish and dying.” He closed his white eyes wearily, then forced them open once again. “I should not have left the Wall. Lord Snow could not have known, but I should have seen it. Fire consumes, but cold preserves. The Wall . . . but it is too late to go running back. The Stranger waits outside my door and will not be denied. Steward, you have served me faithfully. Do this one last brave thing for me. Go down to the ships, Sam. Learn all you can about these dragons.”

paraphrase: George R.R. Martin has reportedly implied that the Night's King has not survived to the present day, leading the video to conclude Coldhands is probably not the Night's King.
A further theory suggests Coldhands is a descendant of the Night's King and his blue-eyed bride, since human-Other hybrids have been hinted to exist.

Against the hybrid theory: Coldhands doesn't seem part-Other -- Others are described as strange, beautiful creatures, a different sort of life, whereas Coldhands is just a dead man with no blue eyes, icy sword, or other White Walker features.

Even if Coldhands were a hybrid, that wouldn't explain why his mind survived death, and the Night's King's bride seems more like a wight than an Other anyway; the video concludes there's no strong evidence for the hybrid theory.

The video concludes none of the identity candidates are fully convincing, and while it's tempting to think Coldhands is just an anonymous Night's Watchman killed and resurrected, that doesn't explain why he hides his face or why the books keep emphasizing the mystery of his identity -- implying something more is going on.
